coredata.dc:title: A highly selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for doxycycline based on the sensitization effect of bovine serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Lu
Zhao, Yanyu
Li, Huihui
Zhang, Qiujuan
Yang, Weiting
Fu, Bo
Pan, Qinhe
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent probes with in-situ visual feature have received numerous attentions for detecting doxycycline (DC), a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic widely used in animal husbandry. However, reported fluorescent probes commonly fail to selectively detect DC among tetracycline antibiotics due to their structural similarity. In this work, bovine serum albumin-capped gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) were ingeniously used as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics through the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC. After adding DC, the red fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs almost remained unchanged, while the green fluorescence of DC also emerged under the sensitization of BSA. BSA-AuNCs showed the highest response toward DC among tetracycline antibiotics ascribed to the strongest sensitization effect of BSA on DC. BSA-AuNCs also displayed the features of simple synthesis, short response time (1 min) and low detection limit (36 nM). BSA-AuNCs were finally applied to detecting DC in fish samples, and further fabricated into test strips for ease of carrying. Thus, this work proposes an efficient strategy to design fluorescent probe for selectively detecting DC among tetracycline antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxycycline
Tetracycline antibiotics
Ratiometric fluorescent probes
Gold nanoclusters
Bovine serum albumin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of BSA-AuNCs. (B) XPS survey spectrum of BSA-AuNCs. (C) XPS narrow spectrum of Au 4f in BSA-AuNCs. (D) Fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA-AuNCs with excitation at 380 nm, inset: the corresponding photograph taken under the 365 nm beam.
Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-AuNCs before and after adding DC (20 μM) with excitation at 380 nm, inset: the corresponding photographs taken under the 365 nm beam.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-AuNCs after adding diﬀerent concentrations of DC with excitation at 380 nm. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 520/F 650) of BSA-AuNCs with excitation at 380 nm and the concentration of DC in the range of 2–30 μM.
Fluorescence emission spectra of different solutions with excitation at 380 nm, DC-20 μM, BSA-50 μM.
Fluorescence intensity ratios (F 520/F 650) of BSA-AuNCs after adding other tetracycline antibiotics (80 μM) in the absence or presence of DC (80 μM) with excitation at 380 nm.
Photographs of the test strips after immersed in different concentrations of DC under the 365 nm beam.
Mechanism for the ratiometric detection of DC using BSA-AuNCs.
Binding constants and binding-site numbers of BSA with different tetracycline antibiotics.
Comparison between BSA-AuNCs and reported ratiometric fluorescent probes for DC.
Detection of DC in fish samples with BSA-AuNCs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A highly selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for doxycycline based on the sensitization effect of bovine serum albumin

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Science, Hainan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent probes with in-situ visual feature have received numerous attentions for detecting doxycycline (DC), a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic widely used in animal husbandry. However, reported fluorescent probes commonly fail to selectively detect DC among tetracycline antibiotics due to their structural similarity. In this work, bovine serum albumin-capped gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) were ingeniously used as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics through the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC. After adding DC, the red fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs almost remained unchanged, while the green fluorescence of DC also emerged under the sensitization of BSA. BSA-AuNCs showed the highest response toward DC among tetracycline antibiotics ascribed to the strongest sensitization effect of BSA on DC. BSA-AuNCs also displayed the features of simple synthesis, short response time (1 min) and low detection limit (36 nM). BSA-AuNCs were finally applied to detecting DC in fish samples, and further fabricated into test strips for ease of carrying. Thus, this work proposes an efficient strategy to design fluorescent probe for selectively detecting DC among tetracycline antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics, which contain four linearly fused tetracyclic nuclei, are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics produced by streptomyces (Gao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Doxycycline (DC), a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic, possesses better antibacterial activity, stronger penetrating power, wider distribution, higher bioavailability and longer elimination half-life than natural tetracycline antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) (Xu et al., 2019, 2020). As a consequence, DC has been widely applied in animal husbandry as a feed additive to promote the animal growth, while the consequent DC residues in animal-derived foods such as meat, milk and egg cause serious threat to the human health. Previous studies reveal that negative physiological responses can be triggered by the DC residues in animal-derived foods including anaphylactic reaction, gastrointestinal disturbance, hepatotoxicity and so on (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2018; Bai et al., 2020). Thereout, the European Union has established the maximum residue limits for DC in different animal-derived foods, such as 100 µg kg−1 in muscle and 200 µg kg−1 in eggs (Yu et al., 2020). Therefore, it is significant to develop efficient methods for monitoring the concentration of DC in animal-derived foods.

Conventional methods for DC detection include high performance liquid chromatography (Xu et al., 2017), capillary electrophoresis (Deng et al., 2012), electrochemical method (Tang et al., 2018), and so on. Although these methods provide sensitive and precise detection for DC, they require huge amount of time, complicated sample preparation, expensive instruments and highly professional operators (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2018; Bai et al., 2020). Fluorescent probes have received more and more attention in the research field of detection (Ge et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020; Cetinkaya et al., 2019), which can overcome the drawbacks of these conventional methods, and their performances can be further improved via combing with nanomaterials (Li et al., 2019a, 2020a, 2020b; Yang et al., 2020) or regulating their structures (Wu et al., 2016, 2017). Fluorescent probes for DC are generally designed based on the inner-filter effect or the antenna effect (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018, 2019b, 2020c; Jia et al. 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Han et al., 2020). For example, Li et al. (2019b) synthesize NH2-MIL-53(Al) for the fluorescent turn-off detection of DC. The fluorescence of NH2-MIL-53(Al) is quenched in the presence of DC due to the inner-filter effect. Besides, Yu et al. (2020) realize the fluorescent turn-on detection of DC with Eu-In-BTEC. The mechanism relies on the antenna effect of DC toward Eu3+ in Eu-In-BTEC. Among the fluorescent probes for DC, the ratiometric dual-response are more stable compared with the single-response owing to their self-calibration effect (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020). Reported fluorescent probes for DC usually exhibit imperfect selectivity, which cannot distinguish DC from other tetracycline antibiotics due to the structural similarity (Li et al., 2018, 2019b, 2020c; Jia et al. 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Han et al., 2020). For instance, Li et al. report a ratiometric fluorescent probe for DC based on graphene quantum dots and Eu3+, which also displays identical response to TC and OTC (Li et al., 2018). Thus, it still remains a great challenge to develop fluorescent probes that can selectively detect DC over other tetracycline antibiotics.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) generally contain dozens of Au (0 and I) atoms as the cores and sulfhydryl compounds such as bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the capping agents (Luo et al., 2012; Zhong et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2019). AuNCs with the molecular like fluorescence property have been widely employed to construct probes for various substances (Govindaraju et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Bian et al., 2017; Fang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2015; Ran et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Ru et al., 2020). It has been reported that the fluorescence of tetracycline antibiotics is relatively weak while BSA can sensitize their fluorescence (Ghorai et al., 2013; Mukherjee et al., 2013). There exhibit several hydrophobic binding cavities in the three-dimensional structure of BSA (Sun et al., 2017). Tetracycline antibiotics can enter the hydrophobic binding cavities of BSA, limiting their rotation, and the quantum yields of tetracycline antibiotics are higher under the hydrophobic environment (Ghorai et al., 2013; Mukherjee et al., 2013), thus resulting the fluorescence sensitization effect of BSA on DC. In this work, our group further find out that BSA exhibits stronger sensitization effect on DC over other tetracycline antibiotics, which is caused by the higher binding constant of BSA with DC. Thereout, it is speculated that BSA-AuNCs can serve as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics through the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC, where the red fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs serves as the internal reference and the green fluorescence of DC is sensitized with BSA.

Above all, BSA-AuNCs were herein used as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting DC among tetracycline antibiotics through the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC. BSA-AuNCs displayed red fluorescence at 650 nm when excited with the 380 nm beam, while after adding DC, the green fluorescence of DC at 520 nm also emerged owing to the sensitization effect of BSA ( Scheme 1). Other tetracycline antibiotics including TC, CTC or OTC induced lower responses of BSA-AuNCs, which was caused by the lower sensitization effect of BSA on them. BSA-AuNCs also featured simple synthesis, short response time and low detection limit compared with the reported ones. BSA-AuNCs were finally applied to detecting DC in fish samples, and test strips were further fabricated for the convenience of use. Hence, an efficient strategy is proposed in this work to design fluorescent probe for selectively detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics.

All reagents, unless otherwise specified, were purchased from commercial companies and used without further purification. The water used throughout the experiments was ultrapure water. The cations and anions referred to the corresponding nitrate and sodium salts, respectively. Doxycycline (DC), tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENRO) and norfloxacin (NORF) referred to the corresponding hydrochlorides. The solutions of BSA, DC, TC, CTC, OTC, CIP, ENRO, NORF, glucose, sarcosine (Sar), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (Cys), Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, F-, Cl-, I-, PO4 3-, were prepared in water as the solvent. The solutions of sulfadiazine (SD), sulfadimidine (SM2), sulfametoxydiazine (SMD), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ) and sulfadimethoxine (SDM) were prepared with 0.12 M hydrochloric acid as the solvent.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from Tecnai G2 F20 X transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured with ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Absorption spectra were acquired from UV-2700 UV–vis spectrometer (SHIMADZU, Japan).

BSA-AuNCs were synthesized according to a previous report (Xie et al., 2019). Briefly, the aqueous solutions of HAuCl4 (5 mL, 10 mM) and BSA (5 mL, 50 mg mL−1) were mixed under vigorous stirring for 2 min. Subsequently, the aqueous solution of NaOH (0.5 mL, 1 M) was introduced and the resulted solution was stirring at 37 °C for 12 h. The obtained brown solution of BSA-AuNCs was dialyzed against water for 48 h with dialysis bags of 8000–14,000 Da. The purified solution of BSA-AuNCs was stored at 4 °C in the dark for further utilization.

Different volumes of DC solution (200 μM) and the solution of BSA-AuNCs as-prepared were added to the Tris-HCl buffer solution (400 μL, 100 mM, pH 10.0), and subsequently water was added to replenish the volume to 2 mL. The fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions were recorded after incubation for 1 min at room temperature with excitation by the 380 nm beam.

The interaction between BSA and DC was evaluated with the fluorometric method (Chakraborty et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2017). Different volumes of DC solution (200 μM) and the solution of BSA (50 μL, 2 mM) were added to the Tris-HCl buffer solution (400 μL, 100 mM, pH 10.0), and subsequently water was added to replenish the volume to 2 mL. The fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions were recorded after incubation for 1 min at room temperature with excitation by the 290 nm beam. It was worth noting that the fluorescence data had been corrected with Eq. (1) to eliminate the inner-filter effect before further analysis (Chakraborty et al., 2017). The stern-volmer equation (Eq. (2)) and double-logarithmic equation (Eq. (3)) were subsequently employed for analyzing the fluorescence data. The interaction mode between DC and BSA could be identified with the stern-volmer equation (Xie et al., 2017), while the double-logarithmic equation could provide the information of binding constant and binding-site number (Chakraborty et al., 2017).
(1)
F
cor
=
F
obs
×
e
(
A
ex
+
A
em
) / 2
where F cor and F obs are respectively the corrected and measured fluorescence intensities; A ex and A em are respectively the absorbances of the solutions at excitation and emission wavelengths.
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where F 0 and F are respectively the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore before and after adding the quencher; [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher; K sv refers to the stern-volmer quenching constant; K q, equal to K sv/τ 0, represents the quenching rate constant; τ 0 refers to the average fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore (10−8 s for biomolecules).
(3)
lg [ (
F
0
− F ) / F ]
=
lg
K
b
+ nlg [ Q ]
where F 0 and F are respectively the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore before and after adding the quencher; K b and n respectively refer to the binding constant and binding-site number.
The interactions of BSA with other tetracycline antibiotics including TC, CTC and OTC were also investigated through the fluorometric method as described above.

Basa fish was purchased from a local supermarket in Haikou, China, and was pretreated according to the method described in the literature (Yu et al., 2020). In brief, 2 g fish was homogenized in 20 mL water followed by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation then diluted for further use. Fish samples with different spiked concentrations were also prepared as described above except the addition of DC standard.

Filter papers were immersed in the solution of BSA-AuNCs as-prepared for 2 h at room temperature followed by air-drying to fabricate test strips. For visual detection of DC, the test strips were immersed in different concentrations of DC (Tris-HCl, 20 mM, pH 10.0), and their fluorescence under the 365 nm beam was observed after incubation for 1 min at room temperature.

BSA-AuNCs were facilely prepared via a one-pot method using BSA and HAuCl4 as the raw materials. TEM indicates that BSA-AuNCs display a spherical morphology ( Fig. 1A), and the average particle size is counted to be 4 nm (Fig. S1). In addition, five characteristic peaks can be recognized in the survey XPS spectrum of BSA-AuNCs (Fig. 1B), which are respectively assigned to Au 4f, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s (Wang et al., 2019), suggesting the existence of AuNCs and BSA in BSA-AuNCs. Meanwhile, two peaks at 84.1 and 87.6 eV respectively ascribed to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 are observed in the XPS narrow spectrum of Au 4f (Fig. 1C). The binding energy of Au 4f7/2 is located between 84.0 (Au film) and 86.0 eV (Au(I)-thiolate complexes) (Negishi et al., 2005), confirming the coexistence of Au(0) and Au(I) in BSA-AuNCs. Moreover, the bound sulfydryl can be obseved in the XPS narrow spectrum of S 2p as shown in Fig. S2 (Castner et al., 1996), confirming that BSA is combined with AuNCs through the Au-S bond in BSA-AuNCs.

The fluorescence property of BSA-AuNCs was also investigated as shown in Fig. 1D. BSA-AuNCs display red fluorescence emission at 650 nm when an excitation wavelength of 380 nm is used. Noteworthily, it is BSA that induces the weak band at 430 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA-AuNCs, which is attributed to the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emission of BSA (Wei et al., 2007). In addition, the fluorescence stability of BSA-AuNCs was investigated under different conditions, including pH values, radiation times, ionic strengths and temperatures. It is clearly evident from Fig. S3 that BSA-AuNCs suffer little interference from the above factors, confirming the excellent fluorescence stability of BSA-AuNCs.

After the addition of DC, the fluorescence emission band of BSA-AuNCs at 650 nm is almost unchanged while a new fluorescence emission band at 520 nm is observed, resulting in a change of the fluorescence color from red to green ( Fig. 2). Therefore, BSA-AuNCs can serve as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of DC. The experimental conditions including pH value and incubation time were then optimized for better detection performance. As shown in Fig. S4, the maximum response of BSA-AuNCs toward DC is obtained at pH 10.0, and as shown in Fig. S5, the response of BSA-AuNCs toward DC reaches equilibrium within 1 min. Therefore, pH value of 10.0 and incubation for 1 min were employed in the subsequent experiments.

The detection performance of BSA-AuNCs toward DC was then studied under the optimized conditions. With the increase of DC concentration, the fluorescence emission band at 520 nm is enhanced significantly, while the fluorescence emission band at 650 nm is enhanced unexpectedly, which is owing to the overlap between the two fluorescence emission bands ( Fig. 3A). Moreover, it can be observed from CIE chromaticity diagram that the fluorescence color of BSA-AuNCs is gradually changed from red to green with increasing the DC concentration (Fig. S6). In addition, it is clearly evident from Fig. 3B that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 520/F 650) of BSA-AuNCs displays a good linear relationship with the concentration of DC in the range from 2 to 30 μM. Furthermore, the detection limit of BSA-AuNCs for DC is calculated as 36 nM based on the formula 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and k is the slope of the linear fitting.

As mentioned above, the fluorescence emission band of BSA-AuNCs at 650 nm is almost unchanged while a new fluorescence emission band at 520 nm is observed after adding DC (Fig. 2). DC possesses a weak fluorescence emission band at 520 nm with excitation at 380 nm as shown in Fig. 4A, so the new fluorescence emission band observed in the detection process is originated from DC. Noteworthily, the fluorescence emission band of DC at 520 nm is enhanced significantly in the presence of BSA-AuNCs (Fig. 4A), which is ascribed to the sensitization effect of BSA on DC according the literature (Ghorai et al., 2013; Mukherjee et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 4B, DC displays a stronger fluorescence emission band at 520 nm in the presence of BSA, while BSA only possesses a RTP emission band at 430 nm with 380 nm excitation (Wei et al., 2007), further verifying the sensitization effect of BSA on DC. Hence, the sensitization effect of BSA on DC is the reason why BSA-AuNCs can serve as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for DC (Scheme 1).

There exhibit several hydrophobic binding cavities in the three-dimensional structure of BSA (Sun et al., 2017). According to literature, DC can enter the hydrophobic binding cavities of BSA, limiting its rotation, and moreover DC has a higher quantum yield under the hydrophobic environment, which is the reason why BSA can sensitize the fluorescence of DC (Ghorai et al., 2013; Mukherjee et al., 2013). To verify this viewpoint, the interaction between BSA and DC was investigated with the fluorometric method (Chakraborty et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. S7, BSA displays a fluorescence emission band at 335 nm when excited at 290 nm, which is consistent with the literature (Wei et al., 2007), and the fluorescence emission band of BSA is gradually weakened with the addition of DC. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms generally include dynamic quenching and static quenching, and stern-volmer equation (Eq. (2)) is used to evaluate the quenching mechanism (Xie et al., 2017). The fluorescence data of BSA quenched by DC accord well with the stern-volmer equation as shown in Fig. S8. The stern-volmer quenching constant (K sv) is calculated to be 2.881 × 104 L mol−1, and the quenching rate constant (K q) is estimated as 2.881 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1 via K sv/τ 0, which is higher than the maximum scattering collision quenching constant of biomolecules (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), verifying that the fluorescence quenching of BSA by DC is induced by static quenching (Xie et al., 2017). Furthermore, double-logarithmic equation (Eq. (3)) was employed to estimate the binding constant and binding-site number of BSA with DC (Chakraborty et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence data of BSA quenched by DC accord well with the double logarithmic equation. The binding-site number (n) of BSA with DC is estimated to be nearly 1 as listed in Table 1, verifying that DC enters one of the hydrophobic binding cavities of BSA. The binding constant (K b) of BSA with DC is further calculated to be 2.6 × 108 L mol−1 (Table 1). The above results demonstrate the interaction between BSA and DC sufficiently.

The responses of BSA-AuNCs toward other tetracycline antibiotics including TC, CTC and OTC were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, BSA-AuNCs have the strongest response toward DC among tetracycline antibiotics, and other tetracycline antibiotics exhibit little effect on the response toward DC. Moreover, the responses of BSA-AuNCs toward DC in the absence and presence of all other tetracycline antibiotics only have a slight difference (Fig. S10). These results indicate that BSA-AuNCs can selectively detect DC over other tetracycline antibiotics. Such phenomenon is proven related to the distinguished binding constants of BSA with different tetracycline antibiotics. The binding constants between BSA and different tetracycline antibiotics were measured with the fluorometric method (Table 1). It is evident from the data that the binding-site number of BSA with tetracycline antibiotics are all nearly 1 and BSA exhibits the highest binding constant with DC among tetracycline antibiotics, resulting in the strongest sensitization effect of BSA on DC (Fig. S11), as well as the strongest response of BSA-AuNCs toward DC. Therefore, BSA-AuNCs are capable to detect DC over other tetracycline antibiotics through the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC, which is superior than most fluorescent probes for DC (Li et al., 2018, 2019b, 2020c; Jia et al. 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Han et al., 2020). Therefore, this work provides an efficient strategy to design fluorescent probes for selectively detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics. Table 2 further indicates that BSA-AuNCs possess simper synthesis, shorter response time and lower detection limit than reported ratiometric fluorescent probes for DC (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020).

Since BSA-AuNCs are intended for the application in animal-derived foods, the interferences from common biological substances (glucose, Sar, His, Glu, GSH, Cys, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, F-, Cl-, I-, PO4 3-) and other antibiotics (CIP, ENRO, NORF, SD, SM2, SMD, SMZ, SDM) generally used in animal husbandry were also investigated. As shown in Figs. S12 and S13, the responses of BSA-AuNCs toward these biological substances and other animal-applicable antibiotics are negligible, and the presences of these substances have little influence on the response toward DC, verifying the application potential of BSA-AuNCs in animal-derived foods.

BSA-AuNCs were applied to determining DC in fish samples to validate its application potential for monitoring the concentration of DC in animal-derived foods. As listed in Table 3, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the detection results are in the range from 5.90% to 7.98%, and the spiked recoveries of DC from fish samples are determined ranging from 99.3% to 112.0%, suggesting the reliability of BSA-AuNCs for detecting DC in fish samples. Therefore, BSA-AuNCs exhibit the application potential for the detection of DC in animal-derived foods.

For the convenience of use, test strips were fabricated by immersing ﬁlter papers into the solution of BSA-AuNCs followed by air-drying. As shown in Fig. 6, the test strip displays red fluorescence under the 365 nm beam, while its fluorescence color gradually turns to green with increasing the DC concentration. It can be concluded that the test strips are feasible for the nake-eye detection of DC, which provides great convenience in practical application.

There are already some commercially available test strips for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics. The commercial test strips for tetracycline antibiotics are mostly based on competitive inhibition immunochromatography, which can detect various tetracycline antibiotics based on single color depth under the heated condition. By contrast, the test strips in this work can selectively detect DC over other tetracycline antibiotics, exhibiting higher selectivity, and determine DC based on the ratiometric mode with the color change from red to green, exhibiting higher stability, moreover can make function under the condition of room temperature, exhibiting simpler operation.

In summary, BSA-AuNCs were ingeniously applied to the ratiometric fluorescent detection of DC in this work, where the red emission of BSA-AuNCs served as the reference signal and the green emission of DC was sensitized by BSA. Noteworthily, BSA-AuNCs were capable to detect DC over other tetracycline antibiotics attributed to the selective sensitization effect of BSA on DC. The comparison with the reported ones revealed that BSA-AuNCs also exhibited the merits of simple synthesis, short response time and low detection limit. The practical applicability of BSA-AuNCs was verified by detecting DC in fish samples, and test strips were also fabricated with BSA-AuNCs for the ease of carrying. Therefore, this work proposes an efficient strategy to design ratiometric fluorescent probe for selectively detecting DC over other tetracycline antibiotics.

Lu Ding: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-Original Draft, Visualization. Yanyu Zhao: Investigation, Visualization. Huihui Li: Supervision, Project acquisition, Project administration, Writing-Review & Editing. Qiujuan Zhang: Investigation. Weiting Yang: Supervision, Project acquisition, Project administration, Writing-Review & Editing. Bo Fu: Investigation. Qinhe Pan: Supervision, Project acquisition, Project administration, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (219QN154, 2019RC005), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004025, 22061014, 21761010), the Research Start-up Fund Project of Hainan University (KYQD(ZR)1926, KYQD(ZR)1806), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Science of Ministry of Education in Northeast Normal University, and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry in Jilin University (2020-28).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125759.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bai et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhombic-like Al nanosupporter-based fluorescent immunochromatographic assay for the sensitive detection of tetracycline
